title: Designing a Tokenizer
concept: A subword tokenizer must balance vocabulary size against sequence length, finding the sweet spot for the target dataset.

exercises.prompt: Which technique splits text into small, meaningful pieces to help AI process language efficiently?
exercises.explain: A subword tokenizer splits text into smaller fragments (like 'play' and '##ing') to stay efficient.

cards.title: The Golden Middle of AI Reading
cards.body: How do computers read human language? They don't read words like we do. They break them into tiny pieces called tokens. But if the pieces are too small, reading takes forever. If they are too big, the computer's vocabulary list becomes impossibly huge! Let's learn how AI designers find the perfect balance.

cards.title: What is a Subword Tokenizer?
cards.body: A subword tokenizer cuts text into pieces smaller than whole words but larger than single characters. Its main goal is to find the perfect middle ground: keeping the list of known words (vocabulary) compact while keeping the chopped-up sentences (sequence length) short enough for AI to read quickly.

cards.title: The Lego Builder's Dilemma
cards.body: Imagine building a Lego model. If you only use raw, tiny plastic dots (character-level), building a house takes forever (huge sequence length). If you buy a pre-built plastic castle (word-level), you cannot build anything else (huge vocabulary size). A subword tokenizer is like a Lego set with medium-sized bricks, giving you the perfect balance of creativity and speed.

cards.title: Subwords in Action
cards.body: Instead of keeping 'look', 'looks', 'looked', and 'looking' as four separate vocabulary items, a subword tokenizer might break them down into 'look', '##s', '##ed', and '##ing'. This keeps the dictionary small while easily handling variations!

cards.title: Is Bigger Vocabulary Always Better?
cards.body: Many people think that a bigger vocabulary is always better for an AI. Actually, a massive vocabulary uses too much computer memory and makes training incredibly slow. Finding a smaller, smarter vocabulary is much more efficient!

cards.title: Finding the Sweet Spot
cards.body: Let's compare the extreme ends of tokenization to see why the subword approach is the sweet spot.

left.text: Tiny vocabulary size (just letters), but extremely long sequence lengths. Very slow to process.

right.text: Short sequence lengths, but massive vocabulary size. Hard to store in memory and fails on new words.

exercises.prompt: What is the main downside of splitting text down to individual characters (like 'c', 'a', 't')?

options.text: It makes the vocabulary list way too large to store.
It makes the sequence length extremely long, slowing down the model.
It makes it impossible for the model to learn grammar.
It prevents the model from reading numbers.

exercises.explain: If you only tokenized by individual characters, your vocabulary would be tiny (just the alphabet), but a single sentence would turn into a massive sequence of dozens of tokens, slowing down the AI.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Subword tokenizers help AI models handle new or rare words that weren't in their official dictionary.
exercises.explain: A subword tokenizer can break unfamiliar words into familiar subwords, allowing it to understand spelling variations and new terms without failing.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about text splitting.
exercises.sentence: To balance vocabulary size and sequence length, AI models use a _ tokenizer.
exercises.explain: A subword tokenizer splits text into pieces smaller than whole words to keep both vocabulary size and sequence length in check.

exercises.prompt: Which tokenized output shows the work of a typical subword tokenizer?
exercises.explain: Sentence A represents a typical subword tokenization output where common roots and endings are split. Sentence B is a character-level split, and Sentence C is a word-level split.

exercises.prompt: Why do AI engineers carefully design and tune the vocabulary size of a subword tokenizer?

options.text: To translate words directly into English without using numbers
To balance vocabulary size and sentence length for efficient AI training
To encrypt secret messages so humans cannot read them
To remove all punctuation and spaces from a text dataset

exercises.explain: A subword tokenizer is designed specifically to strike a balance between having too many unique vocabulary items and having sentences that are too long for the model to process efficiently.
